FT_IC1.evidence: Secondary analysis of ABCD Study data, methods confirm enrollment of youth aged 9-10 at baseline at 21 U.S. sites

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: longitudinal logistic regression (GEE method) testing associations between phone ownership/social media use at age 11-12 and substance use outcomes over 18 months

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Addictive Behaviors, 2025

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 identified: phone ownership (binary yes/no) and social media use (binary yes/no) are phone/SM-specific variables paired with individual-trait outcomes (alcohol, nicotine/tobacco, cannabis use). OR = 1.34 [95% CI 1.18, 1.51] for phone ownership → alcohol; OR = 1.71 [1.21, 2.40] for phone ownership → nicotine/tobacco; OR = 4.91 [2.84, 8.47] for social media use → cannabis

FT_IC3.evidence: Quantitative results reported: multiple odds ratios with 95% confidence intervals across all substance use outcomes (alcohol, nicotine/tobacco, cannabis)

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analysis with numeric results: Table 2 (phone → alcohol OR 1.34), Table 3 (social media → alcohol interaction), Supplementary Tables 1-4 report logistic regression coefficients and odds ratios for all substance outcomes

FT_EC7_reason: Design is longitudinal but SM/phone variables (phone ownership, social media use) are IVs predicting substance use outcomes (DV), not the reverse. EC7 carve-out does not apply because SM is not DV-only in all analyses
notes: Strong candidate for inclusion; phone ownership and social media use measured at age 11-12 predict substance use 18 months later

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study sample: 4557 adolescents (mean age 9.955±0.164 years) from NIH-funded U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study across baseline, 1-year, 2-year, and 3-year follow-ups

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: linear mixed models and cross-lagged panel models examining associations between screen media activity and mental health with brain developmental patterns as mediators

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in International Journal of Clinical and Health Psychology, 2025

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 found no phone/SM-specific variable with standalone coefficient. Step 2 composite analysis: 'Screen media activity' includes 6 types: television, videos, video games, texting, video chat, and social media. Composite bundles TV, video games (unspecified platform), and phone/SM items without separate breakout per methods (3 types of video, generic video games). Methods state 'SMA includes 6 types only bounded by hours on six distinct types of SMA spent on six different types of activity': TV, video games, texting, video chat, social media, and online dating. Multi-item composite includes TV and unspecified video games without isolation; therefore NOT_MET

notes: Excluded due to FT-IC2 failure: composite screen media activity includes TV and video games without separate phone/SM isolation

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study population: youth aged 11 to 12 years (N = 10,052) from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: multilevel regression models predicting internalizing, externalizing, suicidal ideation, and non-suicidal self-injury from screen time and problematic media use

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Development and Psychopathology, 2025

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 found no phone/SM-specific variable. Step 2 composite analysis: Screen media activity and problematic media use are composites. Per methods, parents and youth reported 'how much time youth spend on six types of SMA spent on six different types of activity': watching/streaming TV shows or movies, watching/streaming videos, playing video games, texting, video chat, social media. Composite bundles TV, unspecified video games, and phone/SM items without separate breakout. TV and console/PC gaming are included in composite without isolation per methods description. NOT_MET

notes: Excluded due to FT-IC2 failure: composite screen time and problematic media use include TV and video games (unspecified platform) bundled without separate phone/SM isolation
